title: Active Learning: Smart Labeling
concept: Active learning is a technique where the model selects the most uncertain or informative images for humans to label.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a common method to identify noise in labels?

options.text: Flagging samples where the model's prediction differs heavily from the human label with high confidence
Running vertical graph fusion on the neural network layers
Saving all annotations in YOLO text format instead of JSON

exercises.explain: Comparing high-confidence predictions with labeled answers helps spot likely labeling mistakes.

cards.title: Labeling only what matters
cards.body: If you want to train a self-driving car, you don't need to label 10 million pictures of empty, straight highways. It's a waste of time and money. Instead, you want to find and label the rare pictures of construction zones, dogs crossing, or heavy snow.

cards.title: Active Learning
cards.body: Active Learning is a machine learning framework where the model interactively queries a human annotator to label new data points. It selects images that lie near the decision boundary or have high prediction uncertainty.

cards.title: A Smart Study Plan
cards.body: Imagine preparing for a test. A bad student tries to read every single page of the dictionary. A smart student does a practice quiz, highlights the concepts they got wrong or found confusing, and studies only those. Active learning is that smart study plan.

cards.title: Uncertainty Sampling
cards.body: An unlabeled pool of 1 million road images is fed through a model. The model outputs a confidence score for each. It discards images with 99% confidence (standard roads) and extracts images with 50% confidence (unusual intersections) for human review.

cards.title: We don't need to label everything
cards.body: You might think you must label every image you collect to get the best model. In practice, labeling only the most informative 10% of images using active learning can achieve 95%+ of the accuracy of a fully-labeled dataset at a fraction of the cost.

cards.title: Random vs. Active Selection
cards.body: How data selection strategies spend labeling budgets:

left.text:
Label random images from pool.
Waste budget on identical, easy images.
Slow accuracy growth.

right.text:
Model picks high-uncertainty images.
Focuses budget on hard edge cases.
Rapid accuracy growth.

exercises.prompt: What is the core idea behind Active Learning?
exercises.explain: Active learning optimizes human effort by labeling only the images the model is most uncertain about.

options.text: The model identifies which unlabeled images are most confusing and requests human labels for them
The model continuously trains on a GPU without needing human intervention
The model automatically generates synthetic images to expand the training set
The model changes its activation functions dynamically based on loss

exercises.prompt: True or False: Active learning requires you to label every single collected image in the pool.
exercises.explain: False. Active learning aims to achieve high performance by labeling only a small, informative subset of data.
Images near the decision boundary have the highest prediction uncertainty, making them the most valuable to label.
exercises.sentence: Active learning typically selects images that lie closest to the model's decision ___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a standard metric used to select images in active learning?
exercises.explain: Prediction entropy measures the model's uncertainty, letting it select ambiguous samples for human review.

options.text: Prediction entropy or low class confidence
Image height and width in pixels
The file format of the annotations (.xml vs .json)

exercises.prompt: You are building a face detection system. You have 10,000 labeled images and 1,000,000 unlabeled images. How do you implement Active Learning?

options.text: Train a model on the 10,000 images, run inference on the unlabeled pool, extract the images with the lowest detection confidence, and send them to humans for labeling.
Label all 1,000,000 images randomly to ensure perfect variety.
Discard the 10,000 labeled images and train only on the unlabeled data.
Use the model to label all 1,000,000 images without checking them.

exercises.explain: By training a baseline model, predicting on the unlabeled set, and labeling only the lowest-confidence inputs, you build a highly targeted training loop. Next up: analyzing model failures!
